refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles agreed with customer). Measure end-to-end packet loss across Telemetry and Tasking interfaces during simulated and live teleoperation scenarios. Document network conditions and acceptance evidence.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on operator control.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds safe thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication paths (dual network links) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission on Telemetry and Tasking streams, tuned to customer network profile.
DP-C: Establish quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to protect critical teleoperation packets from congestion-induced loss.
DP-D: Combine DP-B and DP-C: implement FEC/retransmission with QoS prioritization as a layered resilience strategy.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a layered communication resilience architecture combining adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission on Telemetry and Tasking streams, integrated with QoS prioritization and traffic shaping to protect critical teleoperation packets under customer-defined network conditions.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation packet loss mitigation to network infrastructure (QoS capability) and to operator alerting logic (graceful degradation). These are acceptable couplings: QoS is a standard network service, and operator alerting is a safety function that must be coordinated.
No circular dependency detected: FEC/retransmission logic is independent of QoS configuration; QoS is independent of FEC algorithm selection.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is preferred over DP-A (redundant paths) because dual network links introduce cost and operational complexity without addressing packet loss on individual links. DP-D is preferred over DP-B or DP-C alone because layered resilience (FEC + QoS) provides defense-in-depth: FEC handles random loss, QoS handles congestion-induced loss.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer network supports QoS configuration (DSCP marking, traffic prioritization). Risk: If customer network does not support QoS, DP-D effectiveness is reduced; mitigation: validate QoS capability during network assessment phase.
Assumption: FEC overhead (bandwidth increase) is acceptable within customer network capacity. Risk: FEC adds 10–30% overhead; mitigation: tune FEC parameters to customer bandwidth profile.

Assumption: Operator alerting logic (FR-NGHE-009.3) is implemented in Safety Services. Risk: If Safety Services does not own operator alerting, coordination overhead increases; mitigation: confirm Safety Services ownership in IM assignment.

Risk: High safety criticality requires validation that FEC/retransmission latency does not exceed operator control loop requirements. Mitigation: measure end-to-end latency under FEC/retransmission load.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound. Recommend adding explicit latency budget allocation: FEC encoding/decoding and retransmission buffering must not exceed [X] ms to preserve operator control responsiveness. This should be flowed down to IM-level design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication pipeline, including FEC/retransmission logic, QoS integration, packet loss detection, and operator alerting coordination. Teleoperation interfaces with Vehicle (Tasking output, Telemetry input), Safety Services (SafetyAlert coordination), and Network Link (physical transmission).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Incoming sensor/state data from Vehicle; subject to FEC decoding and packet loss detection within Teleoperation module.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outgoing operator commands to Vehicle; subject to FEC encoding and QoS prioritization within Teleoperation module.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional coordination with Safety Services: Teleoperation reports packet loss events; Safety Services triggers operator alerts and graceful degradation policies.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical network transmission layer; Teleoperation configures QoS parameters and monitors link quality metrics.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of FEC/retransmission logic, packet loss detection, and QoS integration for teleoperation streams. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert coordination (operator alerting) requires external dependency, which is appropriate for safety-critical handoff.

Vehicle is a remote asset; it should not own teleoperation communication resilience logic. Placing FEC/retransmission in Vehicle would duplicate logic across multiple vehicle instances and complicate maintenance.

Safety Services owns operator alerting and graceful degradation policies, but should not own FEC/retransmission algorithms. Mixing communication resilience with safety logic violates separation of concerns.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container; it could own teleoperation resilience as a cross-cutting service. However, Teleoperation is a more cohesive and testable boundary. NGHE Solution should orchestrate Teleoperation and Safety Services interaction, not implement FEC/retransmission directly.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it encapsulates all teleoperation-specific communication logic (FEC, retransmission, QoS), (2) it owns both Telemetry (in) and Tasking (out) interfaces, (3) it minimizes diffusion by centralizing packet loss detection and mitigation, and (4) it provides a clear, testable boundary for acceptance testing against the ≤1% packet loss criterion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module is not given authority to configure QoS on Network Link, FEC/retransmission alone may be insufficient. Mitigation: ensure Teleoperation has read/write access to Network Link QoS parameters.

Risk: If operator alerting logic is not coordinated with Safety Services, Teleoperation may trigger alerts that conflict with safety policies. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert interface contract (packet loss threshold, alert priority, graceful degradation actions) in collaboration with Safety Services.

Risk: If Vehicle does not reliably report Telemetry, Teleoperation cannot detect packet loss on the return path. Mitigation: require Vehicle to include sequence numbers and timestamps in Telemetry for loss detection.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has explicit ownership of: (1) FEC encoder/decoder, (2) retransmission buffer and timeout logic, (3) packet loss detection and reporting, (4) QoS parameter configuration on Network Link, and (5) SafetyAlert interface contract. Document these responsibilities in the module interface specification.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (layered FEC + QoS resilience) directly addresses the refined FR (packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions). The DP is specific, measurable, and achievable. No ambiguity in the mapping: FEC/retransmission reduces random loss, QoS reduces congestion-induced loss, and together they target the ≤1% acceptance criterion. Coupling is acceptable (network infrastructure dependency is standard).

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: SafetyAlert coordination requires Teleoperation to communicate packet loss events to Safety Services. This is a necessary coupling for safety-critical alerting; mitigation is to define a clear, minimal SafetyAlert interface contract (e.g., packet loss threshold, alert priority).

Diffusion risk: QoS configuration requires Teleoperation to interact with Network Link. This is acceptable because QoS is a standard network service; mitigation is to provide Teleoperation with a stable, well-documented Network Link API.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment for DP-D. The 5% diffusion (SafetyAlert coordination) is appropriate and necessary for safety-critical systems. No alternative module (Vehicle, Safety Services, NGHE Solution) provides better containment without introducing higher diffusion or violating separation of concerns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: packet loss can be measured end-to-end on Telemetry and Tasking interfaces under controlled network conditions. Acceptance testing can simulate customer network profiles (latency, bandwidth, loss rate) and measure whether DP-D achieves ≤1% effective packet loss. Teleoperation module boundary is clear and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns packet loss mitigation (DP-D implementation). Vehicle owns Telemetry generation (with sequence numbers for loss detection). Safety Services owns operator alerting policy. Network Link owns QoS configuration. No ambiguity in responsibility boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Refine the SafetyAlert interface contract: define packet loss threshold (e.g., 2% instantaneous loss triggers alert), alert priority (e.g., high), and graceful degradation actions (e.g., reduce command frequency, switch to manual mode).
Specify FEC parameters (code rate, block size) and retransmission timeout as tunable configuration, not hardcoded. This allows adaptation to customer network profiles.

Add latency budget: FEC encoding/decoding + retransmission buffering must not exceed [X] ms (e.g., 100 ms) to preserve operator control loop responsiveness. Flow this down to Teleoperation detailed design.
Require Vehicle to include sequence numbers and timestamps in Telemetry for packet loss detection. Document this in the Telemetry interface specification.

decision.justification: The refined FR is sound, DP-D is well-justified and decoupled, and Teleoperation is the optimal IM boundary with 95% containment. However, the FR is not atomic: it decomposes into three sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, graceful degradation). Each sub-FR requires detailed design and verification. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to allocate DP-D logic across sub-FRs and to detail the SafetyAlert and Network Link interfaces.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time (via sequence number tracking and timeout detection).
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission to minimize effective packet loss impact on operator control.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall coordinate with Safety Services to gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds safe thresholds.

assumptions: Customer network supports QoS configuration (DSCP marking, traffic prioritization).
FEC overhead (10–30% bandwidth increase) is acceptable within customer network capacity.
Vehicle reliably generates Telemetry with sequence numbers and timestamps for loss detection.
Safety Services owns operator alerting policy and graceful degradation logic.
Network Link provides stable API for QoS parameter configuration.
Operator control loop latency budget is [X] ms; FEC/retransmission must not exceed this budget.
open_risks: High safety criticality: FEC/retransmission latency must be validated against operator control loop requirements. Mitigation: measure end-to-end latency under FEC/retransmission load during detailed design.
Customer network assessment: QoS capability and bandwidth profile must be confirmed before DP-D implementation. Mitigation: conduct network assessment phase with customer.

SafetyAlert interface contract: packet loss threshold, alert priority, and graceful degradation actions must be defined in collaboration with Safety Services and Safety Manager. Mitigation: schedule interface design review with Safety Services team.
Teleoperation module scope: ensure Teleoperation has authority to configure QoS on Network Link. Mitigation: confirm module responsibilities and API access in architecture review.
evidence_needed: Network assessment report: customer network profile (latency, bandwidth, loss rate, QoS capability).
FEC/retransmission algorithm selection and parameter tuning rationale (code rate, block size, timeout).
Latency budget allocation: operator control loop latency requirement and FEC/retransmission latency contribution.
SafetyAlert interface specification: packet loss threshold, alert priority, graceful degradation actions.
Teleoperation module interface specification: Telemetry (in), Tasking (out), SafetyAlert (inout), Network Link (inout).
Acceptance test plan: packet loss measurement under representative network conditions, acceptance criterion ≤1%.
